perturbation.original_text: 3.5 Distributor has no authorization to make, and will not make, any guarantee or warranty with respect to any Product — all Product warranties are to be communicated to Customers directly by Tripath in writing. Distributor will make no representations as to quality, performance, capabilities, and the like except as are expressly authorized in writing by Tripath (such as in Tripath's standard, published specifications for a Product).
perturbation.changed_text: 3.5 Distributor has full authorization to make guarantees or warranties with respect to any Product, regardless of whether such warranties are communicated to Customers directly by Tripath in writing. Distributor may make representations as to quality, performance, capabilities, and the like, without express authorization in writing by Tripath.
perturbation.explanation: This modification gives the distributor the power to make warranties and representations about the product without Tripath's approval. This creates a potential conflict and legal uncertainty regarding product liability and warranty claims.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change directly contradicts UCC § 2-313, which governs express warranties. By allowing the distributor to make warranties without Tripath's authorization, the contract introduces ambiguity about which warranties are valid and enforceable, potentially conflicting with the manufacturer's intended warranties and creating liability issues.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. (1) Express warranties by the seller are created as follows: (a) Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise. (b) Any description of the goods which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the description. (c) Any sample or model which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the whole of the goods shall conform to the sample or model. (2) It is not necessary to the creation of an express warranty that the seller use formal words such as "warrant" or "guarantee" or that he have a specific intention to make a warranty, but an affirmation merely of the value of the goods or a statement purporting to be merely the seller's opinion or commendation of the goods does not create a warranty. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the principle outlined in UCC § 2-313, as referenced in Scraped Law Snippet 1. The original contract prevents the distributor from making warranties without Tripath's authorization. The changed text grants the distributor 'full authorization' to make warranties, which introduces ambiguity and potential conflict regarding valid and enforceable warranties. This conflicts with the manufacturer's intended warranties, potentially creating liability issues. Snippet 1 states that 'Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty,' meaning the distributor's unauthorized warranties could be legally binding, thus contradicting the original intent and potentially violating the law.

perturbation.original_text: 16.1 Confidential information ("Information") of Tripath and/or Distributor will mean information that the disclosing party desires to protect against unauthorized use or disclosure, and which is furnished under this Agreement as follows: (a) written or other tangible information clearly marked with a notice, such as -5- Distributor Agreement "CONFIDENTIAL"; and (b) oral information that is identified at the time of disclosure as being confidential. Confidential Information will not include information that: (a) enters the public domain without a breach of this Agreement, (b) is known to the recipient prior to the time of disclosure, or is independently developed by the recipient without using any Information, or (c) is obtained from another source that the receiving party has no reasonable cause to believe is under any obligation of confidentiality with respect to Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 16.1 Confidential information ("Information") of Tripath and/or Distributor will only mean information that the disclosing party explicitly states is not confidential, regardless of whether it is marked or identified as such. Confidential Information will include all information regardless of its prior availability, independent development, or source, and despite any obligation of confidentiality from another source.
perturbation.explanation: This modification flips the definition of confidential information, making nearly all information considered confidential regardless of whether it should be, and severely restricts what is considered non-confidential. This creates a contradiction that could cause disputes over intellectual property, trade secrets and proprietary information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts the definition of 'trade secret' under the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). CUTSA defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known. The modified contract's definition eliminates the requirement that confidential information possess economic value or be generally unknown, which broadens the scope of confidentiality beyond what is legally protected as a trade secret under California law. This discrepancy could invalidate confidentiality claims.

UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines confidential information, stating it only means information explicitly stated as not confidential, contradicting the generally accepted definition of trade secrets and confidential information. The modified text also includes all information regardless of prior availability, independent development, or source, and despite any obligation of confidentiality from another source which contradicts the definition of a trade secret. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from the California Civil Code, references the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which protects information deriving independent economic value from not being generally known. The modified contract eliminates these requirements, broadening the scope of confidentiality beyond legal protections, thus creating a direct legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 21.7 Arbitration. Any dispute or claim arising out of this Agreement will be referred to and resolved by the International Chamber of Commerce ("ICC") in accordance with the ICC Arbitration Rules. The venue for such arbitration will be Santa Clara County, California, USA.
perturbation.changed_text: 21.7 Arbitration. Any dispute or claim arising out of this Agreement will be referred to and resolved by a coin flip conducted by the parties. If they cannot agree on the flip, disputes will be resolved by trial in Guam.
perturbation.explanation: Replaced a standard arbitration clause with a coin flip, and the possibility of a trial in Guam. This makes the contract unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts the Federal Arbitration Act (FAA). The FAA requires that arbitration agreements be written and that they provide a mechanism for resolving disputes. Replacing a well-defined arbitration process with a coin flip or trial in Guam undermines the enforceability of the arbitration agreement and potentially conflicts with the FAA's intent to promote arbitration as a means of dispute resolution.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 2 - Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate U.S. Code Notes prev | next A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising out of such a contract, transaction, or refusal, shall be valid, irrevocable, and enforceable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract or as otherwise provided in chapter 4. (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 670 ; Pub. L. 117–90, § 2(b)(1)(A) , Mar. 3, 2022 , 136 Stat. 27 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 2, 43 Stat. 883 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2022— Pub. L. 117–90 applicable with respect to any dispute or claim that arises or accrues on or after Mar. 3, 2022 , see section 3 of Pub. perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a standard arbitration clause with a coin flip and potential trial in Guam, directly conflicting with the Federal Arbitration Act (FAA). The FAA, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, emphasizes the validity, irrevocability, and enforceability of 'written provision[s] in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration.' By replacing arbitration with a coin flip, the changed text undermines the enforceability and recognized mechanisms for dispute resolution. The original text adheres to standard arbitration practices, while the changed text deviates significantly, rendering it likely unenforceable under the FAA. This falls under misaligned terminology, because the new text does not utilize any formal or legally binding dispute resolution practices.
